item.title: USMCA Debate Heats Up as Transshipment Data Soars
item.description: Heated rhetoric between the US and Canada sets stage for tough negotiations

item.title: Dow Rises as Higher Prices Help Revenue Guidance Beat Estimates
item.description: Dow Inc. shares rose after the company issued a better-than-expected revenue outlook, as supply disruptions caused by the Middle East conflict led to higher prices for its products.

item.title: Pakistan Clings to Hope for US-Iran Talks as Public Grows Weary
item.description: Businesses and residents in Pakistan’s capital are growing frustrated with the city’s weeks-long lockdown of roads, schools and offices as authorities continue to keep security restrictions in place in the hope of hosting further US-Iran peace talks.

item.title: Air France-KLM, Lufthansa Bids Advance in Race for TAP Stake
item.description: Portugal has invited Air France-KLM and Deutsche Lufthansa AG to submit binding bids for a minority stake in TAP SA, setting up a contest for one of Europe’s last remaining state-owned airlines.

item.title: Keurig Dr Pepper Revenue Beats Estimates, Buoyed By Cold Drinks
item.description: Keurig Dr Pepper Inc’s quarterly revenue and earnings beat expectations as strong sales of cold beverages and in international markets offset a decline in coffee.

item.title: Child Safety Officers Need Bigger Budgets to Protect Kids From Online Abuse
item.description: Easier-to-use AI tools have sparked an increase in abusive imagery

item.title: Comcast Profit Beats Estimates With Fewer Broadband Losses
item.description: Comcast Corp. reported first-quarter financial results that exceeded analysts’ estimates with fewer losses among broadband customers, offsetting lackluster growth at its Peacock streaming service.

item.title: How the Murder of Bob Lee Fueled Tech’s Rightward Swing
item.description: The 2023 murder of a tech executive seemed to fit neatly into narratives about street crime in liberal cities, until it didn’t.

item.title: Flag Football Teams Are a New Pitch for Small Colleges to Lure Students
item.description: Flag football is having a breakout moment — and colleges across the US are leaning in, investing in full-blown programs to attract student athletes and raise their profiles by backing one of the fastest-growing high school sports.

item.title: Kevin Warsh Triggers Debate Over Measures of US Inflation
item.description: The nominee to chair the Fed says data that’s being used to judge consumer prices is imperfect.

item.title: Rerouted US Imports Avoiding Trump’s Tariffs Top $300 Billion
item.description: About $300 billion worth of goods subject to Trump administration tariffs are avoiding the levies annually and reaching the US from Southeast Asia and Mexico, exposing enforcement vulnerabilities just as a review of the North American trade deal is set to begin.

item.title: Dangote Agrees to Help East Africa Build Refinery in Tanzania
item.description: Kenyan President William Ruto said his government and neighboring Uganda are in talks with Africa’s richest man to build a crude oil refinery in Tanzania, as the war in Iran exposes the continent’s over-reliance on fuel imports from the Middle East.

item.title: Central Banker Says Orban Regime ‘Blackmail’ Made Him Quit Erste
item.description: A former Hungarian central bank governor and critic of Viktor Orban said he quit a top job at Erste Group Bank AG following “blackmail” from the financial regulator in Budapest.

item.title: Blackstone Sees ‘Best Year Ever’ for Its IPOs as Earnings Beat
item.description: Blackstone Inc. reported a larger-than-expected jump in distributable earnings, boosted by a robust start to dealmaking before the war in Iran rattled investors. With US markets rising and AI ventures preparing to go public, President Jon Gray predicted the firm’s “best year ever” for stock listings.

item.title: Japan’s Katayama Reiterates Constant Close Contact With US on FX
item.description: Japan’s Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama said officials are in close contact around the clock with their US counterparts on the foreign exchange market, as Tokyo remains on alert over speculative moves that are keeping the yen weak.

item.title: Sri Lanka Probes $2.5 Million Hack of Finance Ministry Funds
item.description: Sri Lanka is probing the theft of $2.5 million by hackers who breached the Finance Ministry’s computer systems, Treasury Secretary Harshana Suriyapperuma said on Thursday.

item.title: Anglo Advances Coal Business Sale After Setback With Peabody
item.description: Anglo American Plc has at least three potential buyers for its Australian steelmaking coal business after a sale to Peabody Energy Corp. was derailed last year following a fire at one of the mines.

item.title: Sweden Considers Ways to Cut Energy Use If Iran War Continues
item.description: Sweden may need to restrict how much energy its citizens can use, if supplies from the Middle East remain disrupted by the war in Iran, Prime Minister Ulf Kristersson said.

item.title: Tencent Unveils AI Model in High-Stakes Test for OpenAI Hire
item.description: Tencent Holdings Ltd. revealed a major upgrade to its foundational model, marking the first high-stakes test for China’s most valuable company to assert its place in a frenetic AI race since it recruited a top researcher from OpenAI.

item.title: Foundering: A Brilliant Programmer and a Lot of Wild Living
item.description: After his murder, Bob Lee’s friends and family felt that his story was obscured by waves of headlines, rumors, and misinformation, first about street crime in San Francisco, then about the debaucherous lifestyle of the city's elite.

item.title: Netflix Plans to Buy Back Additional $25 Billion in Shares
item.description: Netflix Inc. announced plans to buy back $25 billion in stock after the company’s shares dropped following a weak financial outlook.

item.title: Gilts Slump as Buoyant Economic Data Backs Case for Rate Hikes
item.description: Gilts slumped after a rebound in the UK’s private sector backed the case for the Bank of England to raise interest rates this year.

item.title: Euro-Zone Economy Strained as War Impact Ripples Across Globe
item.description: The global economy just endured a second month of renewed price pressures but with less synchronized fallout on growth, according to the first take of business surveys for April.

item.title: UniCredit Raises Generali Stake After Earlier Signaling Cut
item.description: UniCredit SpA boosted its stake in Assicurazioni Generali SpA to 8.7%, despite earlier indications that it planned to trim the holding.

item.title: Thai IPO Pipeline Faces Difficulties, Exchange President Says
item.description: Thailand’s initial public offering pipeline continues to face challenges, even as signs of renewed investor interest have started to emerge, according to Stock Exchange of Thailand President Asadej Kongsiri.

item.title: Dip Buyers Are Running Back to Stocks Despite $100 Oil
item.description: The reflex to buy stocks is kicking back into gear.

item.title: Taiwan to Ease Limits on Active Funds’ Investments in TSMC
item.description: Taiwan’s financial regulator plans to ease the limit on how much funds can invest in a single stock, lifting an impediment that has prevented local money managers from taking full advantage of the surge in Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co.’s shares in recent years.

item.title: Honeywell Is Said to Near Sale of Warehouse Services Unit to AIP
item.description: Honeywell International Inc. is in advanced discussions to sell its warehouse and workflow solutions business to American Industrial Partners, according to people familiar with the matter.

item.title: Intel’s $240 Billion Rally Slams Into a Potential Earnings Wall
item.description: Intel Corp. has been one of the hottest stocks in the market over the past 12 months, soaring 235% to the highest price since the dot-com bubble. But the rally is facing a potential roadblock in the company’s first-quarter earnings report due after the close Thursday.

item.title: China’s Major Credit Rating Firms to Meet on Improving Quality
item.description: China’s major credit‑rating firms are set to meet next week to discuss quality issues at the urging of regulators, according to people familiar with the matter.
